title: Towards Implementable Quantum Divide and Conquer: A TSP Solver with Improved Exponential Base over Held-Karp
authors: Xujun Bai, Yun Shang, Honghong Lin
abstract: The traveling salesman problem (TSP) is a significant classical NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem. In this work, we demonstrate that combining classical dynamic programming with quantum search can yield an achievable quantum advantage for TSP on the basis of excellent work by the authors of~\cite{ambainis2019quantum}. We design the quantum divide and conquer strategy to provide a parameterized spectrum for this combination. The hybrid algorithm proposed in~\cite{ambainis2019quantum} corresponds to a specific case in this spectrum, while the two extremes of the spectrum represent the purely classical Held-Karp and the purely quantum search algorithm, respectively. Within our parameterized spectrum, we prove that the optimal query complexity is $O^*(1.865666\ldots^n)$, achieved with the 4-subset scheme, while the counting in~\cite{ambainis2019quantum} overlooked half of the recursive branches. The correct query complexity of their algorithm is $O^*(2.225880\ldots^n)$ at their chosen parameter ($α\approx0.055362$), and cannot fall below $O^*(2^n)$ for any $α$ - meaning their $8$-subset scheme, correctly analyzed, never surpasses the classical Held-Karp bound.   Furthermore, in previous studies on quantum advantages for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, researchers focused only on improvements in query complexity. Our work, however, points out that the quantum advantage stems not only from the quadratic speedup of quantum search but also from the structured quantum state preparation. We argue that structured state preparation is indispensable for realizing the oracle operator while maintaining the total time complexity of $O^*(1.865666\ldots^n)$. Therefore, we design an elegant method for preparing the set partition state, which makes our TSP solver practically executable.
summary: This paper develops a quantum divide-and-conquer algorithm to solve the TSP, aiming for an exponential speedup over classical methods. It achieves this by designing an efficient structured quantum state preparation technique that encodes set partitions and minimizes the required query complexity to $O^*(1.865666\dots^n)$. The work is significant because it provides a detailed theoretical framework showing how to overcome previous limitations in analyzing quantum advantage for combinatorial optimization.
main_problem: The paper addresses the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem, aiming to find a quantum algorithm with an improved exponential base over classical bounds.
main_result: The authors propose a quantum divide-and-conquer strategy achieving an optimal query complexity of $O^*(1.865666\dots^n)$ for TSP, which they claim surpasses the corrected analysis of previous quantum approaches and is superior to the classical Held-Karp bound.
method: The method combines classical dynamic programming with a novel quantum search component implemented via a structured state preparation technique (set partition state) and utilizing QFT shortcuts in simulation.
model_or_system: The model is abstract, focusing on qubit manipulation to encode set partitions and Hamiltonian cycles. The system involves constructing complex quantum circuits using controlled gates, Dicke states, and simulating the oracle operation via a structured state preparation module.
key_observables: Query complexity (the exponential base of the time/query requirement), gate complexity ($O(n^2)$ or $O(n^2 \log n)$), and circuit depth ($O(n)$).
important_parameters: The optimal number of subsets used in the partition ($k=4$) and the associated parameters ($\alpha_i \approx 0.315742$).
main_assumptions: The quantum advantage relies critically on efficiently preparing a structured set partition state, which is assumed to be achievable with polynomial gate complexity despite requiring an exponential number of qubits for full encoding.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the recursive structure of the TSP solver (e.g., Figure A1), the preparation of ordered 4-partitions using quantum circuits (Figure A7), and simulation results comparing complexities for small $n$ (Table II).
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing the classical complexity baseline (Held-Karp), then introducing a parameterized quantum divide-and-conquer spectrum, rigorously correcting the analysis of prior work ([1]), and finally detailing the construction of the state preparation circuits for optimal performance.
why_interesting: It provides deep theoretical insights into achieving genuine quantum speedups for classically hard problems by carefully analyzing the overheads associated with state preparation versus search space reduction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Address transmission for one vertex (four qubits) in our encoding method. For the branches that mark the origins and ends, we only retain those corresponding to subset A.

FIG. 2: The framework of our TSP solver, where the Ind-module represents the set partition state preparation algorithm and the controlled UG-gate is introduced in Appendix G. The part to the right of the red dashed line is Grover search module, which will be executed many times to amplify the amplitude of the optimal solution.
